preFix.title: Air fryer vs oven: the energy comparison
preFix.subtitle: When a small appliance saves electricity and when the maths doesn't support the switch
preFix.excerpt: An air fryer uses 800-1800 W for 10-20 minutes to cook a portion. A fan oven uses 2000-3500 W for 20-40 minutes. For single portions, the air fryer consistently wins: 0.1-0.3 kWh vs 0.4-1.2 kWh. For larger loads or dishes that need even heat distribution, the oven is more efficient per kg of food cooked. This is how to calculate your own comparison.
preFix.sourceNotes: BEAMA (British Electrotechnical and Allied Manufacturers' Association) small appliance efficiency guidance. Energy Saving Trust guidance on kitchen appliance energy use. Which? magazine appliance testing data (small appliance energy comparisons). UK energy data from BEIS household energy use statistics.

content.text: The question 'should I use the air fryer or the oven?' has a consistent answer for single-portion cooking: the air fryer. It is smaller, heats up in 2-3 minutes rather than 10-15, and cooks most food in two-thirds of the oven time. For a household cooking for one or two people most days, switching to an air fryer for meals that fit inside it saves 200-400 kWh per year, worth roughly 55-110 pounds at 2025/26 UK electricity rates of 27p/kWh.
Energy consumed = power (kW) x time (hours). Both the power and the time are lower for an air fryer on small loads.

Worked example: chips for two people. Fan oven at 200°C: 2.5 kW, 15-minute preheat + 25-minute cook = 40 minutes total = 1.67 kWh. Air fryer at 180°C: 1.5 kW, 2-minute preheat + 18-minute cook = 20 minutes total = 0.5 kWh. Saving per use: 1.17 kWh. At 27p/kWh: 32p per use. If this meal is made four times per week, that is 130p per week, roughly 67 pounds per year from this single substitution.
When the oven is more efficient
Cooking a full roast for six people: the oven holds the whole load at once. The energy cost per kg is lower than running several separate air-fryer batches.

Baking bread, cakes, and pastry: these need even, stable heat from all sides over 20-60 minutes. The oven's larger thermal mass and radiant element give more consistent results; the air fryer's strong convection dries out the crust before the interior is fully cooked.
Multiple dishes at once: a large oven can cook two or three dishes simultaneously, sharing the preheat cost. An air fryer runs one load at a time.
How to measure your own appliances

A plug-in energy monitor on the oven ring will not work: the oven connection is hardwired at the consumer unit. To measure oven energy use, note the smart meter reading (or the whole-house monitor reading) before and after cooking, with nothing else running in the kitchen. This is approximate but accurate enough.
For the air fryer: plug an energy monitor into the socket and read the kWh consumed at the end of the cook. Most monitors display cumulative kWh to two decimal places.

Microwave vs oven for reheating a single portion: microwave uses 0.05-0.10 kWh. Oven uses 0.40-0.80 kWh. Microwave wins for any solid food that reheats adequately at microwave power (most leftovers, soup, rice, pasta).

Toaster vs grill: a two-slice toaster uses 0.02-0.03 kWh per four slices. The grill uses 1.0-1.5 kW and needs at least 3 minutes to heat up before browning begins: 0.10-0.15 kWh for the same result. Toaster wins for toast.

Kettle only boiling what is needed: a 3 kW kettle boiling one mug (250 ml) uses roughly 0.03 kWh. Boiling the same kettle full (1.5 litres) uses 0.18 kWh. If only one mug is needed, only boil for one mug.

attrs.fix: Check the rated power on the label. A 1.5 kW, 4-litre air fryer will use less energy per use than a 2.2 kW, 9-litre model. For single-portion cooking, the 4-litre model is the more efficient choice.
attrs.cause: The appliance is running at maximum power for a full cook time; smaller models have higher relative heat loss from their surfaces than larger models
attrs.symptom: Air fryer energy use seems much higher than expected

attrs.fix: Run a dedicated measurement: note the smart meter before and after a specific meal, cooked with nothing else running, to isolate the cooking energy. The comparison must be like-for-like.
attrs.cause: Other loads in the kitchen or household have increased to offset the cooking saving
attrs.symptom: Smart meter shows no reduction after switching to air fryer
